All errors must be logged before propagation.

Log entries must include:

  • Timestamp
  • Severity
  • Component Name
  • Error Type
  • Message
  • Context
  • Stack Trace (for Error and Critical)

docs.title: Error Recovery
docs.text:
Where possible, Atlas should attempt recovery:

  • Retry transient failures (network, file I/O) with exponential backoff.
  • Fall back to alternative Providers if configured.
  • Disable non-critical components and continue running.
  • Report degraded state through Diagnostics.

Recovery attempts must be logged.

Recovery must never mask the original error.

docs.title: Design Rules
docs.text:
Errors must:

  • Be specific (not generic Exception or ValueError).
  • Carry context.
  • Suggest resolution.
  • Be typed (use the hierarchy, not bare strings).
  • Be testable.

Errors must never:

  • Be silently ignored.
  • Expose internal implementation details to end users.
  • Contain secrets or sensitive data.
  • Use print() instead of the Logger.

docs.title: Lifecycle
docs.text:
Status: Draft

Version: 0.1

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Lifecycle defines how Atlas components behave from creation to shutdown.

Every Runtime object follows the same lifecycle.

docs.title: States
docs.text:
Registered

↓

Initialized

↓

Started

↓

Running

↓

Paused (optional)

↓

Stopped

↓

Disposed

docs.title: Lifecycle Events
docs.text:
Each transition should emit an Event.

Example:

Module.Started

Provider.Initialized

Application.Stopped

docs.title: Failure
docs.text:
Initialization failures should prevent a component from entering Running.

Failures should not affect unrelated components.

docs.title: Shutdown
docs.text:
Shutdown should occur in reverse dependency order.

Applications

↓

Modules

↓

Providers

↓

Runtime

docs.title: Design Rules
docs.text:
Lifecycle should be:

Deterministic

Observable

Recoverable

Consistent

docs.title: Future
docs.text:
Potential future additions:

  • Restart
  • Suspend
  • Hot Reload
  • Live Upgrade

docs.title: Worker Manifest Schema
docs.text:
Every Worker must provide a worker.yaml file to declare its identity, Roles, execution policies, capabilities, and language implementation details.

There is exactly ONE canonical manifest format across the entire platform. Solon, Miron, Varsity, and the Runtime all consume this exact file.

docs.title: Schema Draft
docs.text:
version: "1.0"

worker:
  id: string              # e.g. "atlas.worker.sqlite"
  name: string            
  version: string         
  language: string        # e.g. "python", "rust", "wasm"
  roles:                  
    - string              # e.g. "database", "storage"

execution:
  policy: string          # e.g. "singleton", "pool"

communication:
  transports:             # Transports supported by this Worker
    - string              # e.g. "shared_memory", "tcp"
  serialization:          # Serializations supported
    - string              # e.g. "json", "protobuf"

imports:                  
  capabilities:
    - name: string        
      version: string     
      optional: boolean   
      reason: string      

exports:                  
  capabilities:
    - name: string        
      version: string

docs.title: Models
docs.text:
Every project accumulates a few stories.

During Atlas' design, Models were briefly called "Hookers" because they connected Workers to standardized behaviour.

The idea lasted approximately one conversation before everyone realized introducing Atlas with "Workers implement Hookers" was probably not ideal.

The name was retired.

Models are now the authoritative specification Workers implement.

The joke remains part of Atlas lore.

The Lore The term was initially coined because the framework needed a way to "hook" standalone code into a larger operating system context. The engineering team jokingly referred to the declarative rulesets as "Hookers". It was dropped instantly once the marketing implications were discussed, but the "Hooker" naming survives as an internal easter egg.

docs.title: What is a Model?
docs.text:
A Model is the ideal, tool-independent specification that Workers implement.

Models are NOT executable. They are declarative definitions. Multiple tools must be able to consume Models (e.g., Solon, Atlas Studio, the Runtime).

docs.title: Contents of a Model
docs.text: A Model may define: - Interface Definitions: The Python typing.Protocol or RPC schemas expected. - Schemas: Data shapes for inputs, outputs, and storage. - Lifecycle Expectations: Startup, shutdown, and pause states. - Permissions: Data access scopes required by the Model. - Events: The exact payload schemas of events published. - Compatibility Rules: Constraints on runtime execution. - Widgets: UI definitions that Workers implementing the Model must export. - Generators/Tests: Used by Solon to generate scaffolding and mocks.

docs.title: The Rule of Ownership
docs.text:
• Workers implement Models.
  • Workers NEVER own Models.

Models exist independently of any implementation. This allows the Solon developer toolchain to validate any Worker against a Model without executing the Worker itself.

docs.title: Model Versioning Policy
docs.text: Because Models are contracts between independent Workers, they must be strictly versioned using Semantic Versioning (e.g., v1.0.0).

docs.title: Compatibility Rules
docs.text:
• Minor Version Bumps (e.g., v1.0 -> v1.1): Indicate backward-compatible additions (e.g., a new optional capability or event). A Worker requiring v1.0 can safely bind to a Provider exporting v1.1.
  • Major Version Bumps (e.g., v1.0 -> v2.0): Indicate breaking changes to the contract (e.g., changing input schemas, dropping a capability). A Worker requiring v1.0 will not bind to a Provider exporting v2.0 unless a backward-compatibility Adapter (Translator) is explicitly declared.

If a Standard Model evolves and introduces a breaking change, ecosystem stability is preserved because the Runtime enforces these strict version boundaries during Binding negotiation.

docs.title: Registry
docs.text:
The Registry in Atlas is split into two distinct tiers: The Global Registry and the Room Registry.

Neither Registry is a database. They store runtime facts only. Nothing related to business state should ever exist inside a Registry.

docs.title: 1. The Global Registry
docs.text:
The Global Registry is the central source of truth for the entire Atlas Runtime. It tracks the macro-state of the platform.

Stores: - Running Workers - Running Rooms - Available Models - Capabilities - Health metrics - Global Bindings

docs.title: 2. The Room Registry
docs.text:
Every Room owns a local Registry, managed by the Room Steward.

The Room Registry functions as an execution cache. Workers inside the Room communicate through this local Registry rather than repeatedly querying the Global Registry, ensuring massive scalability and reducing locks.

docs.title: Consistency & Recovery
docs.text:
Because the Room Registry is a cache, it can drift from the Global Registry if the ecosystem topology changes (e.g., a Worker dies). The Global Registry is always the definitive source of truth. If a Room is suspended or frozen, the Global Registry retains a checkpoint of the Room's metadata. During recovery, the Room Registry is destroyed and completely rebuilt from the Global Registry's checkpoint. Workers should never treat the Room Registry as durable storage.

Stores: - Room Participants (Workers bound to the Room) - Participant Bindings - Communication Table (Header routing) - Transport Table (e.g., TCP, Unix Socket mapping) - Translation Table (e.g., Python to Rust serializers) - Active Invocations - Outstanding Requests & Pending Responses - Room-local lifecycle information and metrics

docs.title: Roles
docs.text:
Roles are metadata tags applied to Workers.

Roles MUST NOT change runtime execution behavior. The Atlas Runtime treats all Workers equally regardless of their Role.

docs.title: Purpose of Roles
docs.text: Roles are primarily consumed by the ecosystem tooling: - Solon: The developer toolchain (e.g., for generating specific scaffolding). - Atlas Studio: For UI visualization and grouping. - Documentation: For code generation and conceptual organization. - Testing: For categorizing test suites.

docs.title: Indirect Ecosystem Significance
docs.text:
Although the Core Runtime ignores Roles during Invocation routing, Roles have massive indirect significance.

Because Solon and Atlas Studio rely on Roles to understand the architecture of your application, mislabeling a Role will severely impact the developer experience. For example, if you tag a UI component with the database role, Solon will generate incorrect scaffolding (assuming it implements StorageModel), and Varsity will flag the architecture as a violation. Roles are the primary way Atlas enforces semantic correctness in the ecosystem.

docs.title: Common Roles
docs.text:
• manager: A Worker that orchestrates other Workers (e.g., Life, Scholar). Managers exist primarily for organization and Solon tooling.
  • database: A Worker providing persistent data storage (e.g., SQLite, Postgres).
  • storage: A Worker providing object or file storage.
  • cache: A Worker providing ephemeral key-value storage.
  • ai: A Worker providing language model or inference capabilities.
  • network: A Worker handling external communication (e.g., HTTP clients, webhooks).
  • widget: A Worker primarily focused on exporting UI capabilities.

Note: A Worker may define one or multiple Roles simultaneously.

docs.title: Runtime
docs.text:
The Atlas Runtime is the instantiated process of Atlas Core.

The Runtime intentionally remains very small. Its sole purpose is to manage the Global Registry, instantiate Room Stewards, and coordinate lifecycle changes.

docs.title: Execution Flow
docs.text:
1. Boot: The Runtime starts and populates the Global Registry via discovery.
  2. Resolution Check: The Runtime verifies that all Worker dependencies (Capabilities) can be fulfilled.
  3. Room Creation: When an orchestration Worker requests a collaboration, the Runtime spawns a Room Steward to manage that context.
  4. Binding: The Room Steward reads the communication Headers and establishes Sessions between Workers inside the Room.
  5. Invocation: Workers execute Invocations (the requests sent over Sessions).
  6. Shutdown: The Runtime pauses Rooms, drains Invocations, and gracefully terminates.

docs.title: Failure Handling
docs.text:
If a critical Worker fails during execution: 1. Atlas instantly transitions affected Rooms into the Frozen state. 2. The freeze propagates through participating Rooms topologically. 3. Workers are allowed to finish active operations where possible. 4. Recovery procedures are triggered. Execution only resumes once recovery completes.

Room state transitions heavily prioritize graceful recovery over hard crashing.

docs.title: RuntimeInvariants
docs.text:
A Worker never owns another Worker.

A Room never owns business state.

Atlas never executes business logic.

Every Worker is discoverable through the Registry.

Every Session belongs to exactly one Room.

Every Invocation belongs to exactly one Session.

Every runtime decision is explainable from metadata.

Workers never communicate without Atlas-mediated session establishment.

The Global Registry stores facts, not application data.

docs.title: SDK
docs.text:
Status: Draft

Version: 0.1

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
The Atlas SDK enables developers to build Modules, Providers, Applications, and Extensions.

The SDK abstracts Atlas internals.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
The SDK provides:

  • Interfaces
  • Templates
  • Utilities
  • Validators
  • Testing helpers
  • Scaffolding tools

docs.title: Supported Components
docs.text:
Developers should be able to create:

Modules

Providers

Applications

Widgets

Themes

Generators

Extensions

docs.title: SDK Principles
docs.text:
The SDK should be:

Simple

Well documented

Stable

Versioned

Backwards compatible

docs.title: Scaffolding
docs.text:
The SDK should support commands such as:

atlas new module

atlas new provider

atlas new app

atlas new widget

atlas new extension

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
The SDK validates:

Project structure

Manifest

Dependencies

Configuration

Compatibility

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
Every generated component should include:

README

Manifest

Tests

Configuration

Example implementation

docs.title: Design Rules
docs.text:
The SDK should reduce boilerplate.

Generated code should follow Atlas standards.

docs.title: Future
docs.text:
Potential future additions:

  • Visual generators
  • Interactive CLI
  • Marketplace publishing
  • Automatic documentation generation

docs.title: Storage
docs.text:
Status: Draft

Version: 0.1

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
The Storage layer provides persistent data management for Atlas.

Storage is accessed exclusively through the Storage Capability.

No Module may communicate directly with a database.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
• Storage provider independence.
  • ACID support where available.
  • Replaceable implementations.
  • Offline-first.
  • Secure by default.
  • Migration support.

docs.title: Design Principles
docs.text:
Storage is a Capability.

Providers implement Storage.

Modules consume Storage.

Applications configure Storage.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
The Storage layer is responsible for:

  • Persisting data
  • Querying data
  • Updating data
  • Deleting data
  • Transactions
  • Backups
  • Migrations
  • Version compatibility

docs.title: Supported Providers
docs.text:
Initial providers:

  • SQLite
  • PostgreSQL
  • Google Sheets
  • Atlas Cloud

Future providers:

  • MongoDB
  • MySQL
  • CSV
  • JSON
  • Supabase

docs.title: Storage Operations
docs.text:
Every Storage Provider must support:

  • Create
  • Read
  • Update
  • Delete
  • Query

Optional:

  • Transactions
  • Batch Operations
  • Full-text Search
  • Streaming

docs.title: Transactions
docs.text:
Providers should support transactions whenever possible.

If unsupported, Providers must document the limitation.

The Runtime should adapt accordingly.

docs.title: Identifiers
docs.text:
Every stored object must have a globally unique identifier.

IDs must never be reused.

docs.title: Versioning
docs.text:
Storage Providers declare:

  • Schema Version
  • Provider Version
  • Migration Version

Runtime validates compatibility before startup.

docs.title: Design Rules
docs.text:
Storage Providers must:

  • Never contain business logic.
  • Never expose implementation-specific APIs.
  • Validate data before persistence.
  • Produce meaningful errors.
  • Support backups.

docs.title: Future
docs.text:
Planned features:

  • Encryption
  • Compression
  • Distributed storage
  • Read replicas
  • Remote synchronization

docs.title: Technology
docs.text:
Status: Approved

Version: 0.1

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This document records the concrete technology decisions for Atlas.

All implementation must conform to these choices.

Changes to technology choices require an ADR.

docs.title: Language
docs.text:
Python 3.13+

Atlas is implemented entirely in Python.

Rationale:

  • Rich ecosystem for data processing, AI, and automation.
  • Strong typing support through Protocols and type hints.
  • Async/await support for I/O-bound operations.
  • Large developer community.
  • Mature package ecosystem.

docs.title: Runtime Model
docs.text:
Hybrid Async.

The Atlas Runtime uses async/await internally.

All Core Protocols define async methods.

Sync convenience wrappers are provided for contexts that do not require async.

Rules:

  • Core components use async def for all I/O-bound operations.
  • Sync wrappers use asyncio.run() at the boundary only.
  • Never call asyncio.run() from within an async context.
  • Event Bus delivery is async.
  • Module lifecycle hooks are async.
  • Provider operations are async.

docs.title: Package Manager
docs.text:
To be decided.

The repository currently uses uv.lock, suggesting uv as a candidate.

This decision will be formalized in a future ADR.

docs.title: Type Checking
docs.text:
BasedPyright.

All public APIs must pass strict type checking.

docs.title: Linting and Formatting
docs.text:
Ruff (Linter)

Ruff Format (Formatter)

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
pytest.

Rationale:

  • Industry standard for Python testing.
  • Rich plugin ecosystem.
  • Fixture-based dependency injection.
  • Async test support via pytest-asyncio.

docs.title: Interfaces
docs.text:
Python Protocols (typing.Protocol).

Atlas uses structural subtyping rather than nominal inheritance.

Rationale:

  • No coupling between interface definition and implementation.
  • Supports duck typing with static verification.
  • Enables third-party implementations without importing Atlas base classes.

docs.title: Configuration Format
docs.text:
YAML.

Atlas configuration files use YAML syntax.

Rationale:

  • Human-readable.
  • Supports complex nested structures.
  • Widely understood.
  • Good library support (PyYAML, ruamel.yaml).

docs.title: Web Framework
docs.text:
None.

Atlas Core is a library, not a web application.

Applications built on Atlas choose their own surface:

  • CLI
  • FastAPI
  • Django
  • Desktop (Tauri, Electron)
  • Mobile

Atlas Core must remain framework-agnostic.

docs.title: Database
docs.text:
SQLite is the default Storage Provider.

Additional providers (PostgreSQL, Google Sheets, etc.) are optional.

Atlas Core never depends on a specific database technology.

docs.title: Serialization
docs.text:
To be decided.

Candidates:

  • Pydantic (validation + serialization)
  • msgspec (performance)
  • dataclasses + manual validation

This decision will be formalized during Phase 1 implementation.

docs.title: Key Dependencies
docs.text:
The following are expected core dependencies:

  • PyYAML or ruamel.yaml (configuration)
  • aiosqlite (async SQLite)
  • pytest + pytest-asyncio (testing)
  • ruff (linting/formatting)
  • basedpyright (type checking)

Additional dependencies will be introduced per Provider and documented in their manifests.

docs.title: Design Rules
docs.text:
Technology choices should:

  • Prefer standard library when sufficient.
  • Minimize external dependencies in Core.
  • Avoid framework lock-in.
  • Support the hybrid async model.
  • Remain replaceable where practical.

docs.title: Workers
docs.text:
Worker is the ONLY executable primitive in the Atlas architecture.

Everything executable is a Worker. There is NO architectural distinction between "Applications", "Providers", or "Modules".

docs.title: Examples of Workers
docs.text:
• SQLite (Storage Provider)
  • Gemini (AI Provider)
  • Journal (Stateful application feature)
  • Task Manager
  • Life (Manager)

docs.title: Core Responsibilities
docs.text:
Atlas coordinates; Workers execute.

Every Worker may: - Own State: Workers manage their own business state and persistence. - Implement Models: Workers implement the declarative behaviors defined in Models. - Export Capabilities: Workers expose Services, Widgets, Commands, and Events.

Workers never own discovery, routing, or lifecycle coordination.

docs.title: Resource Sharing & Instantiation
docs.text:
Workers are globally managed by Atlas. Atlas never clones Workers automatically; it only follows declared execution metadata.

Worker sharing is determined by three levels of policy: 1. Worker Policy: Does the Worker allow itself to be shared? (e.g., Singleton vs Pool) 2. Runtime Policy: Does Atlas permit sharing globally? 3. Room Policy: Does the specific Room permit shared participants?

Only when all three permit sharing will Atlas reuse a Worker instance across multiple Rooms.

docs.title: Execution Model
docs.text:
Workers do not execute Sessions directly. Every request sent over a Session is translated by Atlas into an Invocation.

Workers execute Invocations based on their declared scheduling metadata (execution.policy). Atlas manages the runtime concurrency model according to these rules:

  1. Singleton: Only one instance of the Worker exists globally. If multiple Invocations arrive concurrently, Atlas queues them. The Worker processes Invocations sequentially. If the queue is blocked beyond the configured system timeout, Atlas rejects new Invocations with a TimeoutError.
  2. Parallel: A single instance of the Worker exists, but Atlas spawns a new execution thread (or coroutine) for each incoming Invocation. The Worker is responsible for internal thread safety and lock management.
  3. Pool: Atlas maintains a fixed pool of Worker instances (e.g., 5 instances of a Database Worker). Invocations are load-balanced across the pool. If all instances are busy, Invocations are queued until an instance becomes available.

docs.title: Language Neutrality
docs.text: Workers may be implemented in any programming language (e.g., Python, Rust, Zig, Go). Language is purely an implementation detail. Workers advertise their supported transports, serializations, and runtime languages in their manifest, allowing Atlas to manage the translation layer during execution.

docs.title: ADR-001: Python with Hybrid Async Runtime
docs.text:
Status: Accepted

Date: 2026-06-28

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Atlas requires a programming language and runtime model for its Core framework.

The runtime model decision affects every interface, Protocol, and Provider in the system.

docs.title: Language: Python 3.13+
docs.text: Atlas is implemented in Python.

docs.title: Runtime Model: Hybrid Async
docs.text:
• The Atlas Runtime uses async/await internally for all I/O-bound operations.
  • All Core Protocol methods use async def.
  • Sync convenience wrappers are provided for simple use cases.
  • asyncio.run() is used only at entry-point boundaries.

docs.title: Why Python
docs.text:
• Rich ecosystem for AI integration (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google SDKs are Python-first).
  • Strong typing via Protocols, type hints, and tools like BasedPyright.
  • asyncio provides native async support without external frameworks.
  • Large community and hiring pool.
  • Mature package ecosystem for all target domains (data, web, automation).

docs.title: Why Hybrid Async
docs.text:
• Atlas Core is I/O-heavy: storage, AI, notifications, and external APIs all benefit from async.
  • Pure async would force all consumers to understand asyncio, increasing adoption friction.
  • Sync wrappers allow simple scripts and CLIs to use Atlas without async boilerplate.
  • The hybrid model follows patterns used by httpx, playwright, and prisma-client-py.

docs.title: Pure Synchronous
docs.text: Simpler but would create performance bottlenecks for AI providers and network I/O. Would require threads for concurrency, adding complexity elsewhere.

docs.title: Pure Async
docs.text: Better performance but higher cognitive overhead. Would make the SDK harder for beginners.

docs.title: TypeScript / Node.js
docs.text: Strong async ecosystem but weaker AI/ML library support. Would limit local model integration.

docs.title: Rust
docs.text: Excellent performance but slower development velocity. Too early for Atlas to trade development speed for runtime speed.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• All Protocols must define async def methods.
  • Testing requires pytest-asyncio.
  • Contributors must understand basic asyncio patterns.
  • Sync wrappers must be maintained alongside async implementations.
  • CodingStandards must include async/await conventions.

docs.title: ADR-002: Capability and Protocol Separation
docs.text:
Status: Accepted

Date: 2026-06-28

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Atlas uses a Provider-based architecture where Modules consume abstract services and Providers implement them.

The relationship between the abstract service identity and the concrete code contract needs to be formally defined.

Two approaches were considered: merging Capabilities and Interfaces into one concept, or keeping them separate.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Capabilities and Protocols are kept as separate concepts with explicit definitions:

  • Capability — A runtime service identity. Discovered, resolved, and injected by the Capability Registry. Example: "storage", "ai", "notifications".

  • Protocol — The code contract (Python typing.Protocol) that implementations must satisfy. Example: StorageProtocol, AIProtocol.

  • Provider — A concrete implementation of one or more Protocols, registered for one or more Capabilities. Example: SQLiteProvider implements StorageProtocol and registers for the "storage" Capability.

docs.title: Relationship
docs.text:
Capability (identity) → references → Protocol (contract)
Provider (implementation) → satisfies → Protocol (contract)
Provider (implementation) → registers for → Capability (identity)

docs.title: Why Separate
docs.text:
• A single Capability can have multiple Protocol versions. As the Storage Protocol evolves from v0.1 to v0.2, both versions can coexist under the same "storage" Capability. This enables backward compatibility.

  • Multiple Capabilities can reference the same Protocol. A "primary-storage" and "backup-storage" Capability could both reference StorageProtocol but resolve to different Providers.

  • Capabilities carry runtime metadata (availability, preferred provider, health status) that Protocols should not.

  • Protocols are static contracts suitable for type checking. Capabilities are runtime constructs suitable for dependency injection.

docs.title: Merged Model
docs.text:
A Capability IS its Protocol. Simpler — one fewer abstraction layer.

Rejected because it conflates the code contract (static, compile-time) with the service identity (dynamic, runtime). This would make version negotiation and multi-provider resolution significantly harder.

docs.title: Service Locator Pattern
docs.text:
Components look up services by name/type from a central registry.

Rejected in favor of explicit Capability injection because service locators hide dependencies, making code harder to test and reason about.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Two separate specification documents must be maintained: Capabilities.md and Interface.md (renamed to Protocols.md for clarity).
  • The Capability Registry must store a mapping from Capability names to Protocol versions and Provider instances.
  • Module manifests declare required Capabilities (not Protocols) — the Registry handles resolution.
  • Protocol definitions live in src/atlas/capabilities/ as Python Protocol classes.
  • Type checkers verify Provider compliance against Protocols at development time.
  • The Capability Registry verifies Provider compliance at runtime.

docs.title: ADR-003: Framework-Agnostic Core
docs.text:
Status: Accepted

Date: 2026-06-28

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Atlas needs to support multiple application surfaces: CLI, web, desktop, and mobile.

The question is whether Atlas Core should embed a web framework (e.g., FastAPI) or remain framework-agnostic.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Atlas Core is a library. It does not embed or depend on any web framework.

Applications built on Atlas choose their own presentation layer:

  • CLI applications use Click, Typer, or argparse.
  • Web applications use FastAPI, Django, Litestar, or any other framework.
  • Desktop applications use Tauri, Electron, or native frameworks.
  • Mobile applications use appropriate mobile frameworks.

Atlas Core provides:

  • Runtime (boot, lifecycle, shutdown)
  • Event Bus
  • Registry and Capability resolution
  • Configuration
  • Module and Provider management

Atlas Core does NOT provide:

  • HTTP routing
  • Request/response handling
  • Template rendering
  • Authentication middleware
  • Session management

These are the responsibility of the Application layer.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Framework lock-in is the #1 risk for a long-lived framework. FastAPI could be replaced by a better alternative in 2 years.
  • Multiple surfaces require multiple frameworks. A CLI app and a web app cannot share FastAPI.
  • Core simplicity. Embedding a web framework adds dependencies, opinions, and complexity that most Atlas applications may not need.
  • The architecture already supports this. Applications assemble Modules and Providers — they can also assemble a web framework.

docs.title: Embed FastAPI
docs.text: Would provide a ready-made web surface but would couple Core to HTTP concerns. Rejected because it violates the "Applications decide their surface" principle.

docs.title: Provide an Optional Web Package
docs.text: A atlas-web package could provide FastAPI integration. This is a valid future direction but not needed for Core.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Atlas Core has no web-related dependencies.
  • The SDK should provide templates for different application surfaces.
  • Example applications should demonstrate how to integrate Atlas with FastAPI, Click, etc.
  • No HTTP-related code exists in src/atlas/core/.

Defines coding standards for Atlas.

Write code for humans first.

3.13+

docs.title: Functions

Functions should:

  • Avoid side effects.

docs.title: Classes

Classes should have one responsibility.

Avoid large "manager" classes.

Prefer dependency injection.

Always use Atlas Logger.

docs.title: Errors

docs.title: Documentation

Keep Core framework-independent.

docs.title: Phase 1.1: Diagnostics & Error Handling Specification
docs.text: The Atlas Runtime relies heavily on deterministic error propagation. Because it is language-neutral, failures in the Python/Rust boundary must be captured as structured, typed errors rather than generic stack traces.

docs.title: 1. Error Hierarchy
docs.text:
All errors derive from a base AtlasError structure.

struct AtlasError {
    readonly code: String;           // e.g., "ERR_MANIFEST_INVALID"
    readonly severity: Severity;     // Fatal | Recoverable | Warning
    readonly message: String;
    readonly context: Map<String, String>; // e.g., {"worker_id": "sqlite", "file": "worker.yaml"}
}

docs.title: Manifest Loader Errors
docs.text:
• ParseError: Invalid YAML/JSON syntax. (Severity: Fatal to Worker)
  • ValidationError: Schema mismatch. (Severity: Fatal to Worker)
  • ResolutionError: Missing base manifest for inheritance. (Severity: Fatal to Worker)

docs.title: Dynamic Loader Errors
docs.text:
• LoadError: OS-level failure (File not found, permission denied). (Severity: Fatal to Worker)
  • MissingSymbolError: Executable lacks the WorkerProtocol interface. (Severity: Fatal to Worker)

docs.title: Registry Errors
docs.text:
• IdCollisionError: Attempting to register an already existing Worker ID. (Severity: Recoverable - Reject registration).
  • LookupError: Capability not found. (Severity: Recoverable - Return empty list).

docs.title: 2. Error Propagation & Boundaries
docs.text:
The Runtime absolutely must not crash because a single Worker failed to load.

Propagation Rule: - Functions internal to a subsystem use native language exceptions or Result types (e.g., Rust Result, Python try/except). - The boundary of every subsystem (its Public API) MUST return a structured Result<T, AtlasError>.

docs.title: 3. Logging & Telemetry
docs.text:
Atlas uses a structured event log rather than text-based print() statements.

enum EventType {
    Boot,
    WorkerDiscovered,
    WorkerLoaded,
    RegistrationFailed,
}

struct TelemetryEvent {
    timestamp: u64;
    event_type: EventType;
    latency_ms: u32;         // Optional, used for load time tracking
    context: String;
}

Key Telemetry Points for Phase 1.1: 1. Load Latency: The Dynamic Loader must measure the time elapsed from invoking the OS loader to returning the WorkerInstance. 2. Registry Size: Emit telemetry every time the Global Registry crosses a threshold (e.g., 100, 1000 workers) to track memory footprint.

docs.title: 4. Recovery Strategies
docs.text: If the Dynamic Loader encounters a syntax error in Python, the recovery hook MUST log the structured error, discard the ExecutableHandle, and immediately yield execution back to the Global orchestrator. Atlas will skip the Worker and continue booting remaining components.

docs.title: Phase 1.1: Dynamic Loader Implementation Specification
docs.text: The Dynamic Loader abstracts OS-level execution contexts. It bridges the gap between the language-neutral Atlas Runtime and language-specific Worker implementations (Python, Rust, Native binaries).

docs.title: 1. Language Abstraction Interface
docs.text:
To support multi-language execution, the loader defines an abstract plugin strategy interface.

interface LanguageLoaderStrategy {
    // Loads the executable into memory and returns a handle.
    load(manifest: WorkerManifest, source_path: String) -> Result<ExecutableHandle, LoadError>;

    // Unloads the code from memory (if supported by the language).
    unload(handle: ExecutableHandle) -> Result<(), UnloadError>;

    // Verifies the entry point defined by the language exists.
    verify_entry_point(handle: ExecutableHandle) -> Result<(), MissingSymbolError>;
}

docs.title: Python Strategy Example
docs.text:
• Uses importlib.util.spec_from_file_location.
  • Requires __init__.py or standard entry module.
  • ExecutableHandle is the loaded Python module object.

docs.title: Native / Rust Strategy Example
docs.text:
• Uses dlopen() (Unix) or LoadLibrary() (Windows).
  • ExecutableHandle is an OS-level pointer.

docs.title: 2. Public API
docs.text:
interface DynamicLoader {
    // Evaluates manifest.language and delegates to the correct strategy.
    load_worker(manifest: WorkerManifest) -> Result<WorkerInstance, LoaderError>;

    // Graceful unload (hot-reload prep).
    unload_worker(worker_id: String) -> Result<(), LoaderError>;

    // Reloads code in-place.
    reload_worker(worker_id: String) -> Result<WorkerInstance, LoaderError>;
}

docs.title: 3. Data Structures & Ownership
docs.text:
struct WorkerInstance {
    readonly id: String;
    readonly manifest: WorkerManifest;

    // The raw pointer or module object. Owned by the DynamicLoader.
    readonly _executable_handle: ExecutableHandle; 

    // The language-agnostic interface exposed to Atlas.
    readonly interface: WorkerProtocol; 
}
Ownership: The DynamicLoader retains strict ownership of ExecutableHandle. If the DynamicLoader drops the handle (e.g., during unload()), the WorkerInstance immediately becomes invalid. The Global Registry holds a weak reference to WorkerInstance.

docs.title: 4. Loading Lifecycle & Failure Recovery
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StrategySelected : Read Manifest Language
    StrategySelected --> Loading : Invoke Strategy
    Loading --> Verifying : Memory Allocated
    Loading --> Error : Missing Binary/Syntax Error
    Verifying --> Loaded : Symbols Match
    Verifying --> Error : Missing Export
    Loaded --> [*]

    Error --> Cleanup
    Cleanup --> [*]

docs.title: Failure Recovery
docs.text: If a Worker fails to load (e.g., syntax error in a Python module, missing .so file): 1. The error is wrapped in a structured LoadError. 2. The Strategy explicitly frees any partially allocated memory (e.g., dlclose()). 3. The Loader returns Err to the caller. The Global Registry is never updated with a broken Worker.

docs.title: 5. Hot Reload & Unloading
docs.text: Hot Reload is handled as a unload -> load sequence. Because Python cannot safely unload modules natively (due to sys.modules caching), the Python strategy will implement a namespace sandbox or require process-level isolation for true hot reloading in future phases. For Phase 1.1, Python unload() acts as a soft-deregister, relying on the GC.

docs.title: 6. Memory Management
docs.text: Memory layout is strictly managed by the Language Strategy. The Core Runtime must never interact with the ExecutableHandle directly; it must always proxy through the WorkerProtocol interface to guarantee memory safety.

docs.title: Phase 1.1: Manifest Loader Implementation Specification
docs.text: The Manifest Loader is responsible for discovering, parsing, validating, and caching worker.yaml files. It guarantees that the Runtime only ever interacts with structurally valid, immutable manifest data.

docs.title: 1. Directory Layout
docs.text:
The loader expects the following structure for any installed Worker: 

/installed_workers
  /worker_name
    worker.yaml         (Required: The canonical manifest)
    /assets             (Optional: Static assets)
    /src                (Required: Executable code)

docs.title: 2. Public API
docs.text:
interface ManifestLoader {
    // Primary entry point. Discovers and loads all manifests in a directory.
    load_directory(path: String) -> Result<List<WorkerManifest>, AggregateError>;

    // Loads a single manifest.
    load_file(path: String) -> Result<WorkerManifest, ParseError>;

    // Returns a cached manifest by Worker ID.
    get_cached(worker_id: String) -> Option<WorkerManifest>;
}

docs.title: 3. Internal API & Parser Architecture
docs.text:
// Stage 1: Raw I/O
_read_bytes(path: String) -> Result<Bytes, IOError>;

// Stage 2: YAML/JSON Parsing (Syntax)
_parse_yaml(data: Bytes) -> Result<RawObject, SyntaxError>;

// Stage 3: Schema Validation (Structure)
_validate_schema(data: RawObject) -> Result<RawObject, ValidationError>;

// Stage 4: Inheritance Resolution (Base Models)
_resolve_inheritance(data: RawObject) -> Result<RawObject, ResolutionError>;

// Stage 5: Hydration (Domain Object)
_hydrate_manifest(data: RawObject) -> WorkerManifest;

docs.title: 4. Internal Data Structures (Immutable Structs)
docs.text:
Once hydrated, the Manifest is deeply immutable. 

struct WorkerManifest {
    readonly id: String;
    readonly name: String;
    readonly version: String; // SemVer parsed
    readonly language: String;
    readonly roles: List<String>;

    readonly execution: ExecutionPolicy;
    readonly communication: CommunicationPolicy;

    readonly imports: List<ImportDefinition>;
    readonly exports: List<ExportDefinition>;
}

struct ImportDefinition {
    readonly capability_name: String;
    readonly version_requirement: String;
    readonly optional: Boolean;
}

struct ExportDefinition {
    readonly capability_name: String;
    readonly version: String;
}

docs.title: 5. Manifest Caching & Thread Safety
docs.text:
State: _cache: Map<String, WorkerManifest>

Thread Safety: The cache is protected by a Readers-Writer Lock (RwLock). - Multiple threads can read the cache simultaneously via get_cached(). - Only the loader thread can acquire a Write Lock during load_directory().

Caching Strategy: Manifests are cached using the absolute file path and SHA-256 hash of the worker.yaml file to prevent duplicate parsing.

docs.title: 6. Sequence Diagram: Load Flow
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller
    participant Loader as ManifestLoader
    participant Disk as FileSystem
    participant Validator as SchemaValidator

    Caller->>Loader: load_file("/path/worker.yaml")
    Loader->>Disk: Read bytes
    Disk-->>Loader: Raw YAML
    Loader->>Loader: Parse to RawObject
    Loader->>Validator: Validate against Schema
    alt Invalid Schema
        Validator-->>Loader: ValidationError
        Loader-->>Caller: Result::Err(ParseError)
    else Valid Schema
        Validator-->>Loader: Validated Object
        Loader->>Loader: Resolve Inheritance
        Loader->>Loader: Hydrate WorkerManifest
        Loader->>Loader: WriteLock Cache -> Insert
        Loader-->>Caller: Result::Ok(WorkerManifest)
    end

docs.title: 7. Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• Disk I/O should be asynchronous or heavily batched.
  • YAML parsing is notoriously slow; the parser should ideally use a C-extension or native parser binding (e.g., libyaml via PyYAML CLoader).
  • Inherited manifests (e.g., extending a base database.yaml) are resolved before caching to avoid runtime lookups.

docs.title: 8. Extension Points
docs.text: • Remote Loaders: Designing _read_bytes behind an interface allows future extension to fetch manifests from a network URL or IPFS.

docs.title: Phase 1.1: Global Registry Implementation Specification
docs.text: The Global Registry is the central repository of macro-state runtime facts. It MUST NOT store business data. It provides O(1) lookups for routing and resolution.

docs.title: 1. Internal Storage & Indexing
docs.text:
The Registry maintains highly optimized, normalized tables (HashMaps).

struct GlobalRegistryState {
    // Primary Key: Worker ID -> WorkerInstance
    workers: Map<String, WorkerInstance>;

    // Primary Key: Room ID -> Room Context (Milestone 1.4)
    rooms: Map<String, RoomInstance>;

    // Index: Capability Name -> List of providing Worker IDs
    capabilities_index: Map<String, List<String>>;

    // Index: Role Name -> List of Worker IDs
    roles_index: Map<String, List<String>>;

    // Health & Stats Tracking
    metrics: Map<String, WorkerMetrics>;
}

docs.title: 2. Lock Strategy & Concurrency
docs.text:
The Global Registry is the most highly contended resource in the platform.

Concurrency Model: - Read-Write Lock (RwLock): - Multiple threads can read simultaneously (get_worker, find_capabilities). - Only one thread can write (register_worker, deregister_worker), which blocks reads temporarily. - Lock Granularity: Fine-grained locks on specific tables rather than one massive lock on GlobalRegistryState to prevent a Room registration from blocking a Capability lookup.

docs.title: 3. Public API
docs.text:
interface GlobalRegistry {
    // Mutators (Write Locks)
    register_worker(instance: WorkerInstance) -> Result<(), RegistryError>;
    deregister_worker(worker_id: String) -> Result<(), RegistryError>;

    // Accessors (Read Locks)
    get_worker(worker_id: String) -> Option<WorkerInstance>;
    get_workers_by_role(role: String) -> List<WorkerInstance>;
    find_providers_for_capability(capability: String) -> List<String>;

    // Health API
    update_worker_health(worker_id: String, status: HealthStatus);
}

docs.title: 4. Internal API & Synchronization
docs.text:
// Synchronizes the capability index when a new worker is registered
_sync_capability_index(manifest: WorkerManifest);

// Prunes dead workers from indices
_clean_indices(worker_id: String);

docs.title: 5. Worker Registration Flow
docs.text: When register_worker is called: 1. Acquire Write Lock on workers. Check for ID collision. 2. Insert into workers. 3. Acquire Write Lock on capabilities_index. Read exports from manifest and append Worker ID. 4. Acquire Write Lock on roles_index. Append Worker ID. 5. Initialize WorkerMetrics with HealthStatus::Starting. 6. Release locks. Broadcast WorkerRegistered internal event.

docs.title: 6. Performance & Scalability Considerations
docs.text:
• O(1) Lookups: By maintaining dedicated HashMaps for capabilities and roles, resolution avoids O(N) iteration over the worker list.
  • Cache Invalidation: The Room Manager will cache resolution results locally. The Global Registry does not push invalidations; Room Stewards poll the Global Registry via quick checksums or version hashes to detect macro-state changes.

docs.title: Phase 1.1: Testing Strategy
docs.text: This document defines the strict testing requirements for the Phase 1.1 components. Testing must guarantee that the core foundation will not collapse under failure or high concurrency.

docs.title: 1. Unit Tests (Isolation)
docs.text: Every subsystem requires absolute isolation testing using mocked boundaries.

docs.title: Manifest Loader
docs.text:
• Valid Schemas: Parse full, valid worker.yaml files.
  • Missing Required Fields: Ensure parsing fails gracefully if id or language is missing.
  • Type Mismatches: Ensure parsing fails if roles is a string instead of a list.
  • SemVer Parsing: Validate strict SemVer formatting on model versions.

docs.title: Dynamic Loader
docs.text:
• Mock Strategy: Test using dummy Python modules.
  • Missing Export: Attempt to load a module that does not export WorkerProtocol. Should yield MissingSymbolError.
  • Syntax Error: Attempt to load a structurally broken Python file. Should yield LoadError without crashing the test runner.

docs.title: Global Registry
docs.text:
• Duplicate Registration: Attempt to register two workers with the same ID.
  • Index Integrity: Register a worker, verify it appears in capabilities_index, then deregister it and verify it is completely purged from all indices.

docs.title: 2. Integration Tests (Handoff)
docs.text:
• End-to-End Load: Pass a directory path to ManifestLoader -> pipe WorkerManifest to DynamicLoader -> pipe WorkerInstance to GlobalRegistry.register_worker().
  • Assert that the Worker is fully accessible and health status is tracked.

docs.title: 3. Concurrency Tests (Thread Safety)
docs.text:
The Global Registry must survive massive parallel access.

Scenario: The Read/Write Flood - Spawn 100 threads. - 10 threads continuously write (registering and deregistering dummy workers). - 90 threads continuously read (querying capabilities). - Success Criteria: No deadlocks, no race conditions, no KeyError during reads while writes are occurring. (Requires strict RwLock implementation).

docs.title: 4. Stress Tests & Benchmarks
docs.text: Benchmark Strategy: - Registry Lookup Speed: Measure latency of find_providers_for_capability() under a load of 10,000 registered workers. Must remain sub-millisecond (O(1) requirement). - Manifest Caching: Measure parsing 1,000 files vs reading from cache.

docs.title: 5. Failure Injection (Chaos)
docs.text:
• Permission Denial: Restrict file read permissions on worker.yaml right before the Manifest Loader attempts to read it. Verify structured IOError propagates cleanly.
  • Mid-Load File Deletion: Delete the executable file immediately after the Manifest Loader parses it, but before the Dynamic Loader runs. Verify LoadError occurs and the Registry state remains clean.

docs.title: Phase 1: Invocation Engine
docs.text: The Invocation Engine introduces Invocations as the fundamental unit of work in Atlas. While the Session Manager governs how workers talk, the Invocation Engine governs what workers are doing. Importantly, it separates the representation of work from the scheduling of work.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Create, track, and destroy Invocations across the entire network.
  • Enforce the strict State Machine Lifecycle.
  • Reconstruct Parent/Child traces (Telemetry).
  • Broadcast Cancellation cascades.

docs.title: Invocation Context
docs.text: When a worker executes an Invocation, it receives an immutable InvocationContext. This context tracks the correlation_id and the root_id. If a worker performs secondary requests (child invocations), Atlas guarantees the root_id and correlation_id propagate down the entire tree.

docs.title: Lifecycle State Machine
docs.text:
Every Invocation enforces strict state transitions. Illegal transitions will immediately raise an InvocationStateError (Severity: RECOVERABLE).

CREATED -> QUEUED -> DISPATCHED -> EXECUTING -> WAITING -> COMPLETED | CANCELLED | TIMED_OUT | FAILED

docs.title: Cooperative Cancellation
docs.text: Cancellation in Atlas is a top-down cascade. If a user cancels a Root Invocation (e.g., they closed the application), Atlas does not just kill the process. Instead: 1. Atlas flags cancellation_requested = True on the Root. 2. The Invocation Engine recursively finds every active child/descendant Invocation in the Registry. 3. Every descendant is flagged for cancellation simultaneously. 4. Workers observe the flag and terminate safely.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
graph TD
    User -->|Creates| A[Invocation A : Root]
    A -->|Spawns| B[Invocation B : Child]
    B -->|Spawns| C[Invocation C : Grandchild]
    B -->|Spawns| D[Invocation D : Grandchild]

    User -.->|Cancels| A
    A -.->|Cascade| B
    B -.->|Cascade| C
    B -.->|Cascade| D

    style A fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#fff
    style B fill:#c0392b,stroke:#fff
    style C fill:#c0392b,stroke:#fff
    style D fill:#c0392b,stroke:#fff

docs.title: Phase 1: Room Manager
docs.text: The Room Manager introduces the Room as Atlas' core execution context. A Room provides a scoped collaboration boundary where Workers interact. Crucially, Rooms never "own" workers; workers remain globally managed and can participate in multiple Rooms simultaneously. Rooms only own Bindings.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Provide a local RoomRegistry execution cache to minimize global lock contention.
  • Expose the Room Steward to enforce the state machine.
  • Manage Freeze / Recovery lifecycles.
  • Enforce nesting limits and global scale constraints.
  • Securely grant Observer bindings to external telemetry systems (Miron).

docs.title: The Room Steward
docs.text: Every Room is managed by an internal RoomSteward. This steward manages the bindings and synchronizes the local RoomRegistry. It ensures that when a worker looks up a capability, it searches its local room context first.

docs.title: Freeze & Recovery Lifecycle
docs.text:
If a critical worker inside a Room fails, Atlas does not crash the entire Room. Instead: 1. The Steward transitions the Room to FROZEN. 2. All outstanding Invocations inside this context are instantly paused. 3. The system waits for an external Recovery Hook (e.g., Miron restarting the worker or rebinding a fallback). 4. Upon recovery, the Steward transitions the Room back to ACTIVE and resumes the invocations.

CREATED -> RESOLVING -> ACTIVE -> FROZEN -> RECOVERING -> DRAINING -> DESTROYED

docs.title: Observer Bindings
docs.text: Miron and other diagnostic systems attach to Rooms via Observer Sessions. The Steward guarantees that any participant bound with is_observer=True has read-only access to the Room Registry and can never mutate state or create new Invocations.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
graph TD
    GlobalRegistry["Global Registry (Workers)"]

    subgraph Room1 ["Room 1 (Active)"]
        Steward1["Room Steward"]
        LocalReg1["Local Room Registry"]
        WorkerA1["Worker A Binding"]
        WorkerB1["Worker B Binding"]
    end

    subgraph Room2 ["Room 2 (Frozen)"]
        Steward2["Room Steward"]
        LocalReg2["Local Room Registry"]
        WorkerA2["Worker A Binding"]
        WorkerC1["Worker C Binding"]
        Observer1["Miron Observer Binding"]
    end

    GlobalRegistry --> WorkerA1
    GlobalRegistry --> WorkerA2
    GlobalRegistry --> WorkerB1
    GlobalRegistry --> WorkerC1

docs.title: Phase 1: Runtime Core (Orchestrator)
docs.text:
The Runtime Core (AtlasRuntime) represents the completion of Atlas Runtime v1. It serves as the supreme orchestrator for all underlying execution primitives (Registry, Workers, Translators, Transport, Sessions, Invocations, and Rooms).

The Core is highly opinionated about lifecycle, but completely agnostic to business logic. It owns none.

docs.title: Boot Sequence
docs.text: Atlas strictly enforces deterministic initialization. If any subsystem fails to boot, Atlas fails fast and enters a FAILED state. Partial booting is explicitly disallowed. 1. GlobalRegistry 2. WorkerManager 3. CapabilityResolver & ExecutionPlanner 4. Transport & TranslationResolver 5. SessionManager 6. InvocationEngine 7. RoomManager

docs.title: Graceful Shutdown
docs.text: Atlas guarantees no state is orphaned. During shutdown(), the Core cascades tear-down signals in the correct dependency order: 1. Rejects new Sessions / Invocations (Scheduler halt). 2. Drains & destroys all Rooms. 3. Closes all Sessions. 4. Shuts down the Transport channel. 5. Flushes the Registry. 6. Enters SHUTDOWN state safely.

docs.title: Public Runtime API
docs.text: The AtlasRuntime object is the single integration point for Atlas Studio, Miron, or any external harness. It deliberately hides the mutative APIs of the subsystems (e.g. RoomSteward, ExecutionPlanner) and only exposes safe, read-only accessor facades to protect structural integrity.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
graph TD
    AtlasRuntime --> GlobalRegistry
    AtlasRuntime --> WorkerManager
    AtlasRuntime --> CapabilityResolver

    subgraph Communication [Communication Layer]
        AtlasRuntime --> SessionManager
        SessionManager --> TranslationResolver
        SessionManager --> Transport
    end

    subgraph Execution [Execution Layer]
        AtlasRuntime --> InvocationEngine
        AtlasRuntime --> RoomManager
    end

docs.title: Phase 1: Session Manager
docs.text: The Session Manager acts as the universal communication primitive in Atlas. It serves as the orchestrator that sits between Workers, utilizing the Transport Layer (to move bytes) and the Translation Resolver (to convert formats), wrapping them into a managed, lifecycle-aware Session.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Provide a thread-safe SessionRegistry for all capability bindings.
  • Expose the Header Protocol so workers can request dependencies en masse.
  • Negotiate and establish connections without relying on internal business logic.
  • Maintain a strict state machine lifecycle for connections.

docs.title: The Header Protocol
docs.text:
Before a Session is established, a Worker emits a CommunicationHeader. Atlas resolves the entire Header before constructing the active Sessions. 

header = CommunicationHeader(
    source_worker_id="DashboardWorker",
    requests=[
        HeaderRequest(capability="database", optional=False),
        HeaderRequest(capability="telemetry", optional=True)
    ]
)

docs.title: Session Negotiation Algorithm
docs.text: When process_header(header) is called: 1. Capability Matching: Atlas finds the best target worker for the requested capabilities via the GlobalRegistry. 2. Translation Negotiation: Atlas asks the TranslationResolver to find the shortest compatible translation chain between the source and target formats. 3. Transport Allocation: Atlas allocates a new channel_id on the TransportStrategy and binds it to the Session. 4. Binding Record: A Binding is created and stored in the SessionRegistry. 5. Lifecycle Progression: The Session transitions from CREATED -> NEGOTIATING -> ESTABLISHED -> ACTIVE.

docs.title: Session Lifecycle State Machine
docs.text:
Every Session enforces strict state transitions. Illegal transitions will immediately raise a SessionStateError (Severity: RECOVERABLE).

CREATED -> NEGOTIATING -> ESTABLISHED -> ACTIVE -> IDLE -> SUSPENDED -> CLOSING -> DESTROYED

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    Worker --> Header: Emits Requests
    Header --> SessionManager: process_header()

    state SessionManager {
        [*] --> Registry: Resolve Capability
        Registry --> Translation: Find Shortest Chain
        Translation --> Transport: Allocate Channel
    }

    Transport --> ActiveSession: Binding Created

docs.title: Phase 1: Translation Layer
docs.text: The Translation Layer acts as a metadata-driven graph orchestrator. Atlas never translates data itself; instead, it dynamically composes Translator Workers into optimal translation chains.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Discover compatible Translator Workers from the Global Registry.
  • Utilize Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm to construct the cheapest path between two arbitrary formats.
  • Remain completely agnostic to the specific data payloads (Atlas only compares format strings like "python" and "json").

docs.title: The Translator Model
docs.text:
A Translator Worker is an ordinary Atlas Worker that advertises its capabilities via TranslationDefinitions in its manifest.

translations:
  - source_format: "python"
    target_format: "json"
    cost: 1

If Worker A (Python) wants to talk to Worker B (C++), and no direct translator exists, Atlas will automatically route the bytes through an intermediate format (e.g., JSON -> Rust -> C++) if that is the cheapest valid path on the Translation Graph.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Worker: Python] -->|python bytes| B[Translator: python -> json]
    B -->|json bytes| C[Translator: json -> cpp]
    C -->|cpp bytes| D[Worker: C++]

    style A fill:#34495e,stroke:#fff
    style D fill:#34495e,stroke:#fff
    style B fill:#1abc9c,stroke:#fff
    style C fill:#1abc9c,stroke:#fff

docs.title: Constraints Checked
docs.text:
• No Hardcoded Languages: Translators use generic abstract strings. python is treated identically to schema-v1.
  • Cache Invalidation: Because graph traversal can be expensive, computed paths are cached. This cache is instantly wiped if a new Translator Worker is installed or removed from the system.

docs.title: Phase 1: Transport Layer
docs.text: The Transport Layer is the foundational communication primitive in Atlas. Its sole responsibility is moving raw bytes from a source to a target.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Deliver payloads asynchronously without blocking the execution thread.
  • Remain entirely agnostic to serialization formats, capabilities, and business logic.
  • Provide a common interface (TransportStrategy) for any future physical transport layer (e.g., TCP, WebSockets, IPC).

docs.title: InMemoryTransport
docs.text: For the v1.0 Python implementation, the primary transport strategy is InMemoryTransport. It utilizes a thread-safe queue.Queue backed by a dedicated dispatcher daemon thread. This guarantees that transport.send() returns instantly (in $O(1)$ time), preventing deadlocks if two workers attempt to send bytes to each other simultaneously.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    WorkerA --> TransportLayer: send(payload)

    state TransportLayer {
        [*] --> Queue: Enqueue Bytes
        Queue --> DispatcherThread: Async Pop
    }

    DispatcherThread --> WorkerB: on_receive(payload)

docs.title: Constraints Checked
docs.text:
• No Serialization: The TransportPayload strictly requires bytes.
  • No Instantiation: The transport layer assumes Workers are already instantiated and listeners are registered by higher orchestration layers.

docs.title: Atlas Standard Library v1 (stdlib)
docs.text:
The Atlas Standard Library is the official collection of reusable Workers that form the foundation of the Atlas ecosystem.

Because Atlas separates execution (Workers) from composition (Managers), this Standard Library is the vocabulary you use to build robust applications. The Library is purposefully small, cohesive, and language-agnostic.

Whether you are using Studio, Miron, Solon, or building a bespoke application, these Workers are the foundational building blocks you will compose.

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text:
• Capabilities over Technologies: We design the Standard Library around what it does, not how it does it. You request a StorageModel, not an SQLiteWorker.
  • Modularity: Every Worker does exactly one thing well.
  • Models First: The Standard Library provides declarative Models. Concrete Workers implement these Models. You should always program against the Model.
  • Language-Agnostic: While the reference implementations are written in Python, the capabilities are universally accessible via Atlas Headers.

docs.title: The Dependency Philosophy
docs.text:
In traditional architectures, if your component needs to save data, you might import an SQLite library:

MyComponent -> SQLite Library

In Atlas, this direct dependency is prohibited. Instead, the dependency flow is inverted through the Model system:

MyWorker -> Storage Model
SQLiteWorker -> Storage Model

When you build a Manager, you compose your application by declaring that your Worker requires the StorageModel, and you provide the SQLiteWorker to fulfill it. This makes your logic instantly compatible with JSON storage, AWS S3, or In-Memory storage without changing a single line of your Worker's code.

docs.title: Catalog Overview
docs.text: The Atlas Standard Library is organized into the following capability categories:

docs.title: 1. Core
docs.text: The essential primitives for application lifecycle and foundational utilities. - Includes: Logger, Configuration, Environment, Settings, Registry Client, Resource Loader, Timer, Scheduler, Clock, Random, UUID.

docs.title: 2. Storage
docs.text: Workers for data persistence, serialization, and caching. - Includes: Filesystem, SQLite, JSON, YAML, TOML, CSV, Binary Storage, Cache.

docs.title: 3. Networking
docs.text: Workers for external communication and internet protocols. - Includes: HTTP Client, HTTP Server, WebSocket, TCP, UDP, MQTT, DNS.

docs.title: 4. System
docs.text: Workers for interacting with the host operating system. - Includes: Process, Terminal, Clipboard, Notifications, Shell, OS Information.

docs.title: 5. UI & Media
docs.text: Workers for graphical interfaces and media manipulation (Optional/Extensions). - UI: Window, Theme, Workspace, File Picker, Dialogs. - Media: Image, Audio, Video, Camera, Microphone.

docs.title: 6. AI & Hardware
docs.text: Workers for machine learning capabilities and physical hardware interfacing. - AI: Embedding, Inference, Tokenizer, Prompt, Vector Store. - Hardware: GPIO, Serial, I2C, SPI, CAN, Motor, Camera, IMU, GPS, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi.

docs.title: SDK Integration
docs.text:
Every worker defined in this catalog can be scaffolded automatically using the Atlas CLI:

atlas new worker my_worker

When you build a project, you can easily pull any of these standard capabilities into your Manager:

# main.py
from atlas_sdk import ManagerBuilder

def build():
    return (
        ManagerBuilder("MyApp", "1.0.0")
        .add_worker("atlas.core.logger")
        .add_worker("atlas.core.config")
        .add_worker("atlas.storage.sqlite")
        .build()
    )

Explore the categories to see the detailed specifications, models, and public APIs available!

docs.title: AI & Hardware Workers
docs.text: The AI & Hardware category represents advanced Atlas capabilities. By standardizing these interfaces, Managers can easily swap between local models, cloud APIs, and physical hardware sensors without rewriting their domain logic.

docs.title: Prompt (atlas.ai.prompt)
docs.text:
Purpose: Formats context, instructions, and user input into standardized prompts. Implemented Model: PromptModel

Public APIs: - format(template: str, variables: dict) -> str - validate(prompt: str) -> bool

docs.title: Tokenizer (atlas.ai.tokenizer)
docs.text:
Purpose: Counts and encodes text into tokens for AI models. Implemented Model: TokenizerModel

Public APIs: - encode(text: str) -> list[int] - decode(tokens: list[int]) -> str - count(text: str) -> int

docs.title: Embedding (atlas.ai.embedding)
docs.text:
Purpose: Converts text into numerical vectors for semantic search. Implemented Model: EmbeddingModel

Public APIs: - embed(text: str) -> list[float] - embed_batch(texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]

docs.title: Inference (atlas.ai.inference)
docs.text:
Purpose: Generates text or actions from an LLM. Concrete implementations could wrap OpenAI, Anthropic, or local LLaMA models. Implemented Model: InferenceModel

Public APIs: - generate(prompt: str, max_tokens: int) -> str - stream(prompt: str) -> iter[str]

docs.title: Vector Store (atlas.ai.vector_store)
docs.text:
Purpose: Specialized storage for semantic search. Implemented Model: VectorStoreModel

Public APIs: - upsert(id: str, vector: list[float], metadata: dict) -> None - search(vector: list[float], top_k: int) -> list[dict]

docs.title: 2. Hardware Workers
docs.text: Hardware workers provide standardized access to physical computing protocols, making Atlas an excellent foundation for robotics, IoT, and embedded systems.

docs.title: GPIO (atlas.hardware.gpio)
docs.text:
Purpose: Controls General Purpose Input/Output pins. Implemented Model: GPIOModel

Public APIs: - setup(pin: int, mode: str) -> None - write(pin: int, value: int) -> None - read(pin: int) -> int

docs.title: Serial, I2C, SPI, CAN
docs.text:
Purpose: Standard communication buses for interacting with microcontrollers and sensors. Implemented Model: BusModel

Public APIs: - write(address: int, data: bytes) -> None - read(address: int, length: int) -> bytes

docs.title: Sensors (IMU, GPS)
docs.text:
Purpose: Reads physical location, acceleration, and orientation. Implemented Model: SensorModel

Public APIs: - read_data() -> dict (e.g., {"latitude": 40.71, "longitude": -74.00})

docs.title: Actuators (Motor)
docs.text:
Purpose: Drives physical movement. Implemented Model: MotorModel

Public APIs: - set_speed(speed: float) -> None - stop() -> None

docs.title: Wireless (Bluetooth, Wi-Fi)
docs.text:
Purpose: Scans and connects to wireless networks and BLE devices. Implemented Model: WirelessModel

Public APIs: - scan() -> list[dict] - connect(device_id: str) -> bool

docs.title: Core Workers
docs.text: The Core category contains the essential utilities for application lifecycle, state management, and foundational primitives. These are the workers that almost every Atlas application will need.

docs.title: Logger (atlas.core.logger)
docs.text: Purpose: Provides a centralized, thread-safe, and context-aware logging mechanism for the entire application. Implemented Model: LoggerModel Roles: [utility, observer]

docs.title: Public APIs
docs.text:
• info(message: str, context: dict = None) -> None
  • warn(message: str, context: dict = None) -> None
  • error(message: str, exc_info: str = None, context: dict = None) -> None
  • debug(message: str, context: dict = None) -> None

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Lifecycle & Configuration
docs.text:
• Executed as a singleton.
  • Config: LOG_LEVEL (default: INFO).

docs.title: Failure Modes & Performance
docs.text:
• Should never throw exceptions that crash the caller. If logging fails (e.g., out of disk space for a file logger), it should degrade gracefully.
  • Operations must be non-blocking.

docs.title: Configuration (atlas.core.config)
docs.text: Purpose: Manages the injection, resolution, and caching of application configuration variables from multiple sources (ManagerBuilder, Environment, .env). Implemented Model: ConfigModel Roles: [utility, provider]

docs.title: Public APIs
docs.text:
• get(key: str, default: str = None) -> str
  • has(key: str) -> bool
  • keys() -> list[str]

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: • May rely on atlas.core.logger for debugging config resolution.

docs.title: Lifecycle & Configuration
docs.text:
• Executed as a singleton.
  • Fully loaded during on_start(). Read-only after boot to guarantee deterministic execution.

docs.title: Clock (atlas.core.clock)
docs.text: Purpose: Provides deterministic time primitives. Essential for testing time-dependent logic without relying on the host OS clock directly. Implemented Model: ClockModel Roles: [utility]

docs.title: Public APIs
docs.text:
• now() -> str (ISO 8601 formatted UTC string)
  • timestamp() -> float (Unix epoch time)
  • sleep(seconds: float) -> None

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Testing Strategy
docs.text: In test environments, the ClockModel is usually mocked by Solon to allow time-travel debugging and instantaneous sleeps.

docs.title: UUID (atlas.core.uuid)
docs.text: Purpose: Generates unique identifiers for entities, messages, or sessions. Implemented Model: UUIDModel Roles: [utility]

docs.title: Public APIs
docs.text:
• v4() -> str (Standard random UUID)
  • v7() -> str (Time-ordered UUID)

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text: Must be highly optimized as it is heavily used by the runtime for Message tracking.

docs.title: Scheduler (atlas.core.scheduler)
docs.text: Purpose: Executes invocations on a recurring basis or after a delay. Implemented Model: SchedulerModel Roles: [orchestrator]

docs.title: Public APIs
docs.text:
• schedule(cron: str, capability: str, payload: dict) -> str (Returns Job ID)
  • delay(seconds: float, capability: str, payload: dict) -> str
  • cancel(job_id: str) -> bool

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: • Requires atlas.core.clock.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: If a scheduled invocation fails, the Scheduler logs the error via LoggerModel but continues running subsequent jobs.

docs.title: Other Proposed Core Workers
docs.text:
• Environment: Reads OS-level environment variables strictly.
  • Settings: Persistent user preferences (usually wraps a Storage worker).
  • Registry Client: Exposes read-only access to the Room Registry for telemetry observers.
  • Resource Loader: Manages static assets bundled within .atlas packages.
  • Timer: Measures execution durations for profiling.
  • Random: Provides deterministic (seedable) random number generation for simulations and games.

docs.title: Networking Workers
docs.text: The Networking category abstracts network protocols, allowing Atlas applications to communicate with the outside world, fetch data, and serve clients.

docs.title: HTTP Client (atlas.network.http_client)
docs.text: Purpose: Fetches data from remote REST APIs and web servers. Implemented Model: HttpClientModel Roles: [network, client]

docs.title: Public APIs
docs.text:
• request(method: str, url: str, headers: dict = None, body: str = None) -> dict (Returns status code, headers, and body).
  • get(url: str) -> dict
  • post(url: str, body: str) -> dict

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: • Often depends on atlas.core.logger to record failed requests.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Must handle timeouts, DNS resolution failures, and connection drops gracefully. Should return a structured error dictionary rather than throwing an unhandled exception.

docs.title: HTTP Server (atlas.network.http_server)
docs.text: Purpose: Serves incoming HTTP requests, allowing an Atlas Manager to act as a web backend. Implemented Model: HttpServerModel Roles: [network, server]

docs.title: Public APIs
docs.text:
• start(port: int) -> None
  • stop() -> None
  • register_route(method: str, path: str, handler_capability: str) -> None

docs.title: Integration with Atlas
docs.text:
When a request hits a registered route, the http_server worker translates the HTTP request into an Atlas Invocation and sends it to the specified handler_capability.

For example, GET /users might trigger the atlas.my_app.get_users capability on another worker. The server waits for the response and translates it back into an HTTP 200 OK.

docs.title: WebSocket Client & Server (atlas.network.websocket_*)
docs.text: Purpose: Full-duplex communication for real-time applications (chat, live dashboards). Implemented Model: WebSocketClientModel, WebSocketServerModel Roles: [network]

docs.title: Public APIs (Client)
docs.text:
• connect(url: str) -> bool
  • send(message: str) -> None
  • on_message(handler_capability: str) -> None

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Like the HTTP Server, WebSocket workers bridge the outside world with internal Atlas capabilities. When a message arrives, the worker invokes the registered handler capability.

docs.title: Other Proposed Networking Workers
docs.text:
• TCP/UDP: Low-level socket programming models for custom protocols.
  • MQTT: IoT messaging protocol, crucial for hardware and robotics managers.
  • DNS: Utility for resolving domain names to IP addresses without relying strictly on the OS default behavior (useful for custom routing).

docs.title: Storage Workers
docs.text: The Storage category provides a unified, declarative interface for persisting data. By using the models in this category, applications can seamlessly switch between memory, local files, and remote databases without changing business logic.

docs.title: Storage Model (atlas.storage.*)
docs.text: Purpose: The foundational contract for key-value and blob storage. Implemented Model: StorageModel Roles: [database, storage]

docs.title: The Contract (Public APIs)
docs.text: Any worker implementing StorageModel must provide: - write(key: str, data: bytes) -> bool - read(key: str) -> bytes (Returns empty bytes or raises an error if not found, depending on strictness) - delete(key: str) -> bool - exists(key: str) -> bool - list(prefix: str = "") -> list[str]

docs.title: 1. Filesystem Storage (atlas.storage.fs)
docs.text: Writes raw bytes to the host OS filesystem. - Dependencies: atlas.core.config (to resolve STORAGE_ROOT_PATH). - Performance: Bounded by OS disk I/O. Use for large blobs (images, documents).

docs.title: 2. SQLite Storage (atlas.storage.sqlite)
docs.text: Stores key-value pairs in a local relational database. - Dependencies: atlas.core.config (to resolve DB_PATH). - Performance: Excellent for concurrent reads. Writes are locked per the SQLite connection.

docs.title: 3. Memory Storage (atlas.storage.memory)
docs.text: Stores data in a volatile Python dictionary. - Lifecycle: All data is lost when the Manager shuts down. - Performance: Ultra-fast. Used for testing and ephemeral caching.

docs.title: Document Storage Model (atlas.storage.document.*)
docs.text: Purpose: While StorageModel handles raw bytes, the Document model handles structured data (JSON/Dictionaries). Implemented Model: DocumentModel Roles: [database]

docs.title: The Contract (Public APIs)
docs.text:
• insert(collection: str, doc_id: str, document: dict) -> bool
  • get(collection: str, doc_id: str) -> dict
  • query(collection: str, filters: dict) -> list[dict]
  • delete(collection: str, doc_id: str) -> bool

docs.title: 1. JSON Storage (atlas.storage.document.json)
docs.text: Saves collections as .json files on disk. - Failure Modes: Susceptible to corruption if the process crashes mid-write. Should implement atomic writes (write to temp file, then rename).

docs.title: 2. YAML / TOML Storage
docs.text: Similar to JSON, but heavily used for user-editable configuration files.

docs.title: Cache (atlas.storage.cache)
docs.text: Purpose: Provides fast, temporary storage with TTL (Time To Live) semantics. Implemented Model: CacheModel Roles: [utility, cache]

docs.title: Public APIs
docs.text:
• set(key: str, value: bytes, ttl_seconds: int) -> bool
  • get(key: str) -> bytes
  • invalidate(key: str) -> None
  • clear() -> None

docs.title: Lifecycle
docs.text: Can be backed by atlas.storage.memory or a dedicated external Redis worker (if provided by a third-party). The standard library implementation uses an in-memory dictionary with a background cleanup thread.

docs.title: Other Proposed Storage Workers
docs.text:
• CSV: Specialized worker for appending row-based data, highly useful for telemetry and data science applications.
  • Binary Storage: Optimized for chunked streaming of large files (video/audio).

docs.title: System Workers
docs.text: The System category abstracts host OS functionality, ensuring that Atlas applications can remain cross-platform (Windows, macOS, Linux) without scattering OS-specific if/else checks throughout the business logic.

docs.title: Process (atlas.system.process)
docs.text: Purpose: Spawns and manages child processes safely. Implemented Model: ProcessModel Roles: [system]

docs.title: Public APIs
docs.text:
• execute(command: str, args: list[str]) -> dict (Returns stdout, stderr, and exit code).
  • spawn(command: str, args: list[str]) -> str (Returns a Process ID for background tasks).
  • kill(pid: str) -> bool

docs.title: Testing Strategy
docs.text: The ProcessModel can be easily mocked using Solon to simulate shell commands without actually running them during CI/CD.

docs.title: Terminal (atlas.system.terminal)
docs.text: Purpose: Advanced CLI interaction, including colors, tables, and progress bars. Implemented Model: TerminalModel Roles: [system, ui]

docs.title: Public APIs
docs.text:
• print(text: str, color: str = None) -> None
  • prompt(question: str) -> str
  • table(headers: list[str], rows: list[list[str]]) -> None

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: Usually implemented under the hood using libraries like rich in Python, but abstracts that dependency away from the rest of the application.

docs.title: Clipboard (atlas.system.clipboard)
docs.text: Purpose: Reads from and writes to the user's system clipboard. Implemented Model: ClipboardModel Roles: [system]

docs.title: Public APIs
docs.text:
• copy(text: str) -> bool
  • paste() -> str

docs.title: Notifications (atlas.system.notifications)
docs.text: Purpose: Triggers native OS desktop notifications. Implemented Model: NotificationModel Roles: [system, ui]

docs.title: Public APIs
docs.text: • notify(title: str, message: str, urgency: str = "normal") -> bool

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: If the host OS does not support notifications (e.g., a headless Linux server), this worker should fail gracefully (returning False or doing nothing) rather than crashing the application.

docs.title: Other Proposed System Workers
docs.text:
• Shell: Evaluates raw shell scripts.
  • OS Information: Retrieves platform details (CPU architecture, memory usage, OS version) for telemetry and diagnostic workers.

docs.title: UI & Media Workers
docs.text: The UI & Media category contains optional extensions for Atlas applications that require graphical interfaces or rich media processing. While Atlas is heavily CLI-first, these workers allow Managers to boot full desktop applications.

docs.title: Window (atlas.ui.window)
docs.text:
Purpose: Creates and manages native OS application windows. Implemented Model: WindowModel

Public APIs: - create(title: str, width: int, height: int) -> str (Returns Window ID) - show(window_id: str) -> None - close(window_id: str) -> None - render(window_id: str, layout: dict) -> None

docs.title: Dialogs (atlas.ui.dialogs)
docs.text:
Purpose: Triggers native OS modal dialogs (alerts, confirmations). Implemented Model: DialogModel

Public APIs: - alert(title: str, message: str) -> None - confirm(title: str, message: str) -> bool

docs.title: File Picker (atlas.ui.file_picker)
docs.text:
Purpose: Opens the native OS file selection dialog. Implemented Model: FilePickerModel

Public APIs: - open_file(allowed_extensions: list[str]) -> str (Returns file path) - save_file(default_name: str) -> str

docs.title: Other Proposed UI Workers
docs.text:
• Theme: Manages light/dark mode transitions and color palettes across the application.
  • Workspace: Manages multiple windows or tabs within a complex application (like Atlas Studio).

docs.title: Image (atlas.media.image)
docs.text:
Purpose: Loads, resizes, and manipulates image data. Implemented Model: ImageModel

Public APIs: - resize(image_bytes: bytes, width: int, height: int) -> bytes - convert(image_bytes: bytes, format: str) -> bytes

docs.title: Camera (atlas.media.camera)
docs.text:
Purpose: Captures still frames or video streams from attached webcams. Implemented Model: CameraModel

Public APIs: - capture_frame() -> bytes - start_stream(fps: int) -> None - stop_stream() -> None

docs.title: Audio & Video (atlas.media.audio, atlas.media.video)
docs.text:
Purpose: Plays and records audio/video files.

Public APIs: - play(file_path: str) -> None - record(duration_seconds: int) -> bytes

docs.title: Microphone (atlas.media.microphone)
docs.text: Purpose: Streams raw audio data for processing (essential for voice-to-text AI workers).
